coredata.dc:title: Reusable tissue-mimicking hydrogel phantoms for focused ultrasound ablation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Ming-Kuan
Shieh, Jay
Lo, Chia-Wen
Chen, Chuin-Shan
Chen, Ben-Ting
Huang, Chang-Wei
Chen, Wen-Shiang
coredata.dc:description: The ability of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM)-based hydrogel phantoms to mimic tissues with different acoustic and thermal properties under high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation was investigated. These phantoms were designed to model the formation of thermal lesions in tissues above the threshold temperature of protein denaturation. By adjusting the concentration of acrylic acid (AAc) in the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms, the cloud point (i.e., lower critical solution temperature) of the phantoms could be tailored to produce HIFU thermal lesions similar to those formed in different swine tissues in terms of size and shape. Additionally, energy thresholds for inducing transient or permanent bubbles in the phantoms during HIFU ablation were also identified to shed light on the onset of cavitation or material damage. The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms developed in this study possess major advantages such as transparent, reusable and tailorable properties, and are practical tools for characterizing an ablative device (or treatment) to determine its efficacy and safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM)
Phantom
Tissue-mimicking
Cloud point
Thermal lesion
High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical images of (a) NIPAM-52 and (b) egg-white-based hydrogel phantoms.
Schematic of the HIFU ablation system.

Evolution of optical transparency and lesion formation of NIPAM-52 hydrogel phantom at different time points during a HIFU heating–cooling cycle (HIFU operating at 23.9W acoustic power; turning on and off at t =61 and 120s, respectively).

Evolution of optical transparency and lesion formation of egg-white-based hydrogel phantom at different time points during a HIFU heating–cooling cycle (HIFU operating at 23.9W acoustic power; turning on and off at t =61 and 120s, respectively).
The (a) area and (b) length of thermal lesions formed in various hydrogel phantoms and swine tissues under HIFU ablation at different acoustic powers for 120s.
Optical images of thermal lesions formed in (a) NIPAM-52 hydrogel phantom, (b) pork tenderloin, (c) swine brain and (d) egg-white-based hydrogel phantom under identical HIFU conditions.

Evolution of the (a) area and (b) length of thermal lesion formed in NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom with increasing AAc volume at an HIFU acoustic power of 30.4W for 120s. Shaded areas in (a) and (b): the area and length of thermal lesions (±1σ) formed in pork tenderloin and swine brain under identical HIFU conditions.

Levels of material damage induced in NIPAM-52 hydrogel phantom under different combinations of HIFU acoustic power and ablation duration. The upper and lower boundary lines indicate the approximate thresholds of inducing permanent and transient bubbles, respectively.

Images of four levels of material damage induced by HIFU in NIPAM-52 hydrogel phantom: (a) No–Bubbles, (b) Bubbles–During–HIFU, (c) Bubbles–After–HIFU and (d) Lasting–Bubbles. (a) and (b) are B-mode ultrasound images; (c) and (d) are optical images.
Constituents of NIPAM-52 and egg-white-based hydrogel phantoms.

head.ce:title: Reusable tissue-mimicking hydrogel phantoms for focused ultrasound ablation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, National Taiwan University Hospital & College of Medicine, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
National Taiwan University Hospital & College of Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Nanomedicine, National Health Research Institutes, Miaoli, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Nanomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Chung Li, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Civil Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Chung Li 32023, Taiwan. Tel.: +886 3 2654206; fax: +886 3 2654299 (C.-W. Huang). Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation, National Taiwan University Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan. Tel.: +886 2 23123456x67087; fax: +886 2 23832834 (W.-S. Chen).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ability of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM)-based hydrogel phantoms to mimic tissues with different acoustic and thermal properties under high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation was investigated. These phantoms were designed to model the formation of thermal lesions in tissues above the threshold temperature of protein denaturation. By adjusting the concentration of acrylic acid (AAc) in the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms, the cloud point (i.e., lower critical solution temperature) of the phantoms could be tailored to produce HIFU thermal lesions similar to those formed in different swine tissues in terms of size and shape. Additionally, energy thresholds for inducing transient or permanent bubbles in the phantoms during HIFU ablation were also identified to shed light on the onset of cavitation or material damage. The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms developed in this study possess major advantages such as transparent, reusable and tailorable properties, and are practical tools for characterizing an ablative device (or treatment) to determine its efficacy and safety.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, less-invasive ablative modalities using thermal energy, such as laser, focused ultrasound, microwave, and radiofrequency ablations, have received considerable attention, especially for localized tumor ablation [1–6]. To expand potential applications and avoid in vivo experiments or human experimentations, the design and processing of transparent tissue-mimicking phantoms capable of demonstrating the evolution and extent of thermal lesion formation in real time are extremely helpful for all ablative devices during preclinical development.

Several temperature-sensitive tissue-mimicking phantoms have been reported as model materials for ablative therapy. For example, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) or agar-based phantoms were used to visualize the effect of bubble-enhanced heating by focused, MHz-frequency ultrasound [7]. However, thermal lesions could not be well visualized in such phantoms. Transparent polyacrylamide (PAA) gels containing bovine serum albumin (BSA) were then proposed since BSA would turn white and optically opaque or reduce the T2 signal on magnetic resonance imaging upon reaching the threshold temperature of protein denaturation [8–10]. Takegami et al. demonstrated a low-cost version by replacing BSA with egg white for the study of focused ultrasound ablation [11]. Although easily fabricated, the major disadvantages associated with egg white or albumin-based tissue-mimicking phantoms are the irreversible protein denaturation and permanent color change above the threshold temperature, making them impossible to be reused.

To solve the above-mentioned disadvantages, new temperature indicators were adopted to replace BSA for the construction of tissue-mimicking phantoms. For example, a nonionic surface-active agent (NiSAA) which exhibited hydrophobic segregation at temperatures above the so called “cloud point” temperature was proposed [12]. The cloud point, also known as the lower critical solution temperature (LCST), represents the temperature when macromolecules transform from a hydrophilic structure (below the cloud point) to a hydrophobic structure (above the cloud point) [13]. For example, polyacrylamide hydrogels containing NiSAA were shown to become opaque when heating above the cloud point, but gradually return to transparent upon cooling [12]. The cloud point of the polyacrylamide hydrogels could be altered by the choice of NiSAA type and further finely adjusted with the addition of methanol or butanol, or by changing the pH value [12].

Similar temperature-sensitive properties to NiSAA can also be found in N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) and polyNIPAM copolymers which possess cloud points near physiological relevant temperatures [13,14]. Our previous study has demonstrated that heating a transparent NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom above the cloud point would lead to the segregation of NIPAM, resulting in the increase of opaqueness at the heating area [16]; moreover, the color (opacity) change is reversible upon cooling, making the phantom a reusable modeling tool. Other major advantages of the NIPAM-based tissue-mimicking phantom are the similarity of its acoustic properties to those of human tissue, and that its threshold temperature (i.e., LCST) could be easily manipulated by the addition of acrylic acid (AAc) [14–16].

This study reports the endeavor to utilize AAc-controlled NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms to mimic the actual shape and size of thermal lesions formed by high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) ablation in different target tissues. Two types of swine tissues, pork tenderloin and brain, with distinct acoustic properties were modeled by the NIPAM-based phantoms for their behaviors under HIFU. The critical energy which may induce transient or permanent bubbles in the phantoms was also characterized to shed light on the cavitation onset or material damage threshold. Due to their reusability and high modeling flexibility, the AAc-controlled NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms would be useful for the rapid characterization and calibration of an ablative device/treatment for different target tissues, and could be used to determine the efficacy and safety of the device/treatment before performing actual ablations.

The NIPAM-based reusable hydrogel phantoms were formed by crosslinking copolymerization of NIPAM and N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAm) with the addition of AAc to adjust the cloud point so that it fell in the temperature range of biological significance [16]. The NIPAM-based phantom with cloud point temperature at 52°C is denoted as “NIPAM-52” phantom in this study. 52°C was chosen since it represents the threshold temperature above which an irreversible tissue damage may occur. The constituents of the NIPAM-52 phantom and their amounts are listed in Table 1 . The fabrication process consisted of the following steps: 0.44ml or volume ratio 0.274% of AAc (99.5%, Acros Organics, USA) was first dissolved in 150ml degassed, distilled water before adding 9g of NIPAM (Acros Organics, USA) to the aqueous solution. The solution was gently stirred at room temperature until complete dissolution of NIPAM. 0.375g of MBAm (97%, Alfa Aesar, UK) and 0.195g of ammonium persulfate (APS; Sigma Chemicals, USA), which acted as the initiator for crosslinking, were then added consecutively into the aqueous solution. The mixture was gently stirred at room temperature until homogenized. Finally, 0.4ml of polymerization agent N,N,N’,N’-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED; 99%, Sigma Chemicals, USA) was added to the mixture. The final aqueous mixture was immediately poured into molding containers and allowed to polymerize completely at room temperature to form hydrogel phantoms as shown in Fig. 1 a. The hydrogel phantoms prepared were either tested in the ablation experiments within 24h after complete polymerization, or stored in an airtight container to avoid dehydration (if left in air) or swelling (if placed in water) for later experimental usage. The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms were optically transparent, gelatin-like materials (see Fig. 1a).

In order to match the shape and size of coagulation lesions in different swine tissues, NIPAM-based phantoms containing different amounts of AAc, ranging from 0.25 to 0.85ml, were fabricated. The cloud point temperature of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom changes with the concentration of AAc. At a fixed ablation power level, a lower AAc concentration would result in a lower cloud point, and thus a larger ablation lesion.

For comparison purposes, traditional egg-white-based tissue-mimicking hydrogel phantoms with ingredients listed in Table 1 were also prepared. The fabrication process consisted of the following steps: 10.5ml distilled deionized water was first mixed into 60ml egg white, followed by adding 4.5ml of anhydrous glycerol (Sigma–Aldrich, USA) into the mixture, which was then gently stirred at room temperature until complete dissolution of glycerol. Next, 24.8ml of acrylamide (Acros Organics, USA) and 0.05g of APS, which acted as the initiator for crosslinking, were added consecutively into the mixed solution. The solution was gently stirred at room temperature until homogenized. Finally, 0.2ml of polymerization agent TEMED was added to the mixed solution, which was immediately poured into molding containers and allowed to polymerize completely at room temperature to form hydrogel phantoms as shown in Fig. 1b. The egg-white-based hydrogel phantoms prepared had a yellowish semi-transparent appearance. They were tested within 24h after complete polymerization, or stored in an airtight container for later experimental usage.

The experimental HIFU system consisted of a function generator (33250, Agilent, USA), a radio-frequency (RF) power amplifier (1040L, Entrepreneurship & Innovation, USA), a power meter (4025, Bird Electronic Corp., USA), and a 2MHz focused piezoelectric transducer (SU-101, Sonic Concepts, USA). The schematic of the HIFU system is shown in Fig. 2 . The HIFU transducer was made of a single element concave piezoelectric ceramic with an aperture diameter of 35mm and a radius of curvature of 55mm, and for the ablation experiments, it was operated in a continuous-wave mode at an electric power of 20–60W, which was calibrated to an acoustic power of 12.3–36.5W by an acoustic force balance (UPM-DT-10AV, Ohmic, Easton, USA). The ultrasound produced by the transducer was aimed at the phantom specimen (5×5×6cm3) fixed in a lucite tank filled with degassed, deionized water. The focal spot of the ultrasound was positioned inside the specimen. The spatial position of the transducer was controlled by a dual axis stepper motor (Jubilee Technology Corp., Taiwan) to an accuracy of 0.1mm.

Differences in the formation of thermal lesions in the NIPAM-52 and egg-white hydrogel phantoms by HIFU ablation were compared first. Changes in specimen color during the ablation experiments were monitored and recorded by a HDV camera at 30 frames per second. The average grayscale pixel values of a fixed region of interest (ROI) of size 4×6 pixels in successive image frames were calculated to reveal the evolution of the optical transparency of the phantom specimen during HIFU ablation. The ROI was located right at the ultrasound focal spot as shown in Fig. 2. The recording of images ran for 240s with the HIFU system turned on at time (t)=61s and off at t =120s. The phantom specimen was allowed to cool naturally back to room temperature after turning off HIFU.

The maximal shape and size of the thermal lesions formed in the NIPAM-52 and egg-white hydrogel phantoms by HIFU ablation at different energies were compared, along with the lesions formed in two types of swine tissues, pork tenderloin and brain. Both types of tissue samples were cut to fit the size of the tissue phantoms, i.e., 5×5×6cm3. The phantom or swine tissue specimens were ablated separately at different electric power levels of 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60W (corresponding to acoustic power levels of 12.3, 18.0, 23.9, 30.4 and 36.5W, respectively) for 120s. In order to reduce the adverse effects of tissue inhomogeneities on the ablation results, we have selected tissue areas with high homogeneity for the ablation experiments; for example, pork tenderloin without tendon or dense fibrous tissues. Apart from the NIPAM-52 phantom (with 0.44ml AAc), other NIPAM-based phantoms with different AAc concentrations – 0.25, 0.35, 0.44, 0.65 and 0.85ml – were also fabricated and tested in order to find phantom systems capable of matching the thermal and acoustic properties of the swine tissues. According to our previous study, the cloud points of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms with AAc concentrations of 0.19 and 0.44ml are 42.4±0.5 and 52.6±0.3°C, respectively [16]. The HIFU ablation experiment was repeated at least three times for each type of phantom or swine tissue specimen for verification purposes.

The magnitudes of ultrasonic power and irradiation duration for the ablation experiments were chosen based on our previous study on the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms – both the acoustic and thermal properties of the phantoms have been determined to be relatively close to those of the swine tissues [16]. Thus, thermal lesions formed in the hydrogel phantoms and swine tissues are comparable.

The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms were designed to be reusable based on that the evolution of their optical transparency with temperature was a stable hysteretic behavior and reproducible in consecutive heating–cooling cycles [16]. Nevertheless, ablating the phantoms at high energies might still lead to material damage, mainly the formation of gaseous bubbles within the focal area. In order to investigate the threshold of material damage under HIFU ablation, an electric power of 40–70W (i.e., 23.9–42.0W acoustic power) was applied to the NIPAM-52 phantom for a period of 30–120s, and damage done to the phantom was assessed. Several levels of material damage (i.e., bubble formation) were defined in this study; with increasing level of damage, these levels are: (1) “No–Bubbles”, meaning no detection of bubbles by a diagnostic probe (7.5MHz, SonoSite Titan, Bothel, USA) throughout the HIFU operation; (2) “Bubbles–During–HIFU”, meaning that bubbles only formed during the operation of HIFU and were visible only by the diagnostic probe; (3) “Bubbles–After–HIFU”, meaning that bubbles were visible by the HDV camera during and after the HIFU operation, but disappeared within 5min of the completion of HIFU ablation; and (4) “Lasting–Bubbles”, meaning that visible bubbles persisted for at least 24h after the completion of HIFU ablation.

Figs. 3 and 4
show the evolutions of the optical transparency and lesion formation of the NIPAM-52 and egg-white hydrogel phantoms, respectively, when being heated up by HIFU with an electric power of 40W (23.9W acoustic power) during the HIFU ablation experiments. The grayscale values of both types of phantoms increased initially with increasing HIFU heating time and then plateaued after opacity saturation was reached. As shown in Fig. 3, the thermal lesion in the NIPAM-52 phantom appeared and grew with HIFU heating time and then gradually disappeared after HIFU was turned off, completing a heating–cooling cycle without causing any residual lesions. The process of changing optical opacity in the NIPAM-52 phantom was reversible but showed a marked hysteresis during a heating–cooling cycle [16]. In comparison, although the optical opacity of the egg-white-based phantom also increased during the initially heating phase, the opacity remained at its maximum value during the cooling phase, as shown in Fig. 4, indicating its irreversibility in the opacity–temperature relationship.

The differences in the final area and length of the lesions formed in different hydrogel phantoms and swine tissues at different acoustic power levels are shown in Fig. 5 . It is evident that in terms of area and length, the lesions formed in the NIPAM-52 hydrogel phantom show a great resemblance to the lesions formed in the pork tenderloin. In contrast, the lesions formed in the egg-white-based phantom are significantly smaller and shorter compared to those in the swine tissues. No thermal lesion was visible in the egg-white-based phantom when the acoustic power of the HIFU transducer was below 23.9W (40W electric power). Note that HIFU ablation of the swine brain tissue was performed only at 30.4W acoustic power – above 30.4W, the highly fragile, soft brain tissue disintegrated due to the high heat; while below 30.4W, the size of the lesion produced was unable to be determined accurately. Fig. 5b shows that the length difference between the thermal lesions in the NIPAM-52 phantom and pork tenderloin is reduced at high acoustic power levels. Besides the similarity in size, the overall shape of the lesions in the NIPAM-52 phantom is also comparable to that in the pork tenderloin, as indicated by the optical images in Fig. 6 . The lesion in the NIPAM-52 phantom is of a capsule-like shape, while in the egg-white-based phantom a typical tadpole shape. It is worth to note that the lesion size in the swine brain tissue is substantially smaller than that in the pork tenderloin; this is likely due to the smaller acoustic attenuation coefficient of the brain tissue [17].

The sizes of the ablation lesions measured in different types of tissues are different due to the different acoustic and thermal properties of the tissues. The adjustable cloud point temperature of the NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom enables us to adjust its lesion size by adjusting the AAc concentration when fabricating the phantom. In this study, the added volume of AAc was varied from 0.25 to 0.85ml in order to match the lesion size produced in a specific swine tissue by HIFU ablation at a fixed acoustic power level. Fig. 7 shows the evolution of the lesion size (in terms of area and length) in the NIPAM-based phantom with increasing AAc volume at an acoustic power of 30.4W. The two shaded areas in Fig. 7 indicate plus and minus one standard deviation of the mean area (Fig. 7a) and length (Fig. 7b) of the thermal lesions formed in the pork tenderloin and swine brain. It is evident that for an AAc volume of about 0.4ml, the size of the thermal lesion (both in area and length) produced in the NIPAM-based phantom fell within the lesion size range of the pork tenderloin under the same acoustic power; while, for an AAc volume of 0.85ml, it fell within the lesion size range of the brain tissue. Note that NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms with AAc volumes above 0.85ml were unable to be fabricated due to the difficulty in achieving successful gelation when the AAc concentration was high.

The tissue-mimicking NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms are reusable for HIFU ablation experiments because of their reversibility in optical opacity during heat-cooling cycles. However, bubbles could form in the phantoms at the focal area under a high acoustic power or a long ablation period, which when present, would change the characteristics of lesion formation. Cavitation bubbles could form during the ablation process and are visible only by a diagnostic ultrasound scanner (i.e., the “Bubbles–During–HIFU” level defined in the previous section). If the bubbles survive without collapsing after the completion of ablation, they may be observed using an optical imaging system and persist for different periods of time (i.e., the “Bubbles–After–HIFU” and “Lasting–Bubbles” levels defined in the previous section). Fig. 8 shows the levels of material damage (i.e., bubble formation) in the NIPAM-52 phantom under different combinations of acoustic power and ablation duration. The upper and lower boundary lines in Fig. 8 represent, respectively, the approximate thresholds of inducing permanent and transient material damages in the NIPAM-52 phantom. The upper boundary, line A, was constructed from the linear regression of minimum acoustic power levels at which bubbles were found 24h after the completion of different time periods of HIFU ablation. Permanent material damage was done to the NIPAM-52 phantom when ablation conditions above line A were applied. In contrast, the lower boundary, line B, was constructed from the linear regression of maximum acoustic power levels at which no bubbles were found both during and after different time periods of HIFU ablation. No material damage was done to the phantom when ablation conditions below line B were applied.

For ablation conditions in between boundary lines A and B in Fig. 8, formation of transient bubbles in the NIPAM-52 phantom was observed; they were visible either only during the HIFU operation or both during and after the HIFU operation, but disappeared within 5min of the completion of HIFU ablation. Fig. 8 shows that at a high acoustic power of 42.0W, material breakage in the NIPAM-52 phantom caused by the formation of permanent bubbles can be expected in an ablation time as short as 30s. On the contrary, when the acoustic power is below 30.4W, no transient or permanent bubbles are induced in the phantom for an ablation time up to 90s. Fig. 9 a–d show, respectively, the images of four levels of material damage induced by the HIFU operation in the NIPAM-52 phantom – “No–Bubbles”, “Bubbles–During–HIFU”, “Bubbles–After–HIFU” and “Lasting–Bubbles”. Fig. 9a and b are the B-mode ultrasound images, while 9c and 9d the optical images. It is evident that no bubbles were detected under short ablation time and low ablation powers (see Fig. 9a). The bright vertical line on the upper part of Fig. 9b (indicated by the arrow) is a cluster of transient cavitation bubbles formed during HIFU ablation; while, the irregular needle-like horizontal lines are the interference acoustic signals picked up by the B-mode ultrasound probe during HIFU ablation. Fig. 9c shows a bubble which persisted after the HIFU experiment, but disappeared within 5min. Fig. 9d shows three permanent bubbles which persisted for more than 24h after the HIFU experiment.

A family of transparent NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms have been developed to overcome the shortcomings of traditional BSA- or egg-white-based phantoms, such as their inability to be reused and unadjustable could point temperatures. Furthermore, a major advantage of the NIPAM-based phantoms over the traditional phantoms is that their acoustic and thermal properties are closer to those of animal and human tissues [16], making them more suitable materials to model the therapeutic effects of HIFU treatments. As shown in our study, the fabrication procedures for the NIPAM-based phantoms are simple with relatively low-cost ingredients. Key features such as reusability and adjustable cloud point temperature and thermal lesion size allow the NIPAM-based phantoms to mimic the behaviors of different tissues under HIFU ablations. It is evident from Figs. 3 and 4 that compared to the egg-white-based phantom, the NIPAM-52 phantom exhibits a higher transparency at room temperature based on the lower grayscale value of its optical image taken before heating. Furthermore, the grayscale value of the image of the NIPAM-52 phantom increases at a faster rate over a wider range of grayscale value during the heating phase, indicating the phantom’s greater ability to differentiate between different stages of lesion formation; in other words, a large opacity (color) change can be achieved with a small temperature variation. Note that a disadvantage of the proposed NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms is the lack of scattering properties, making them not suitable for use in diagnostic ultrasound testing. A possible solution is the addition of low concentration scatters such as glass beads [10]. However, special attention is necessary because adding the scatters may reduce the optical transparency and alter the acoustic attenuation of the phantoms. The volume and overall shape of the HIFU thermal lesions may thus change.

The ultrasound and optical images of the NIPAM-based phantoms indicate that HIFU ablation at high power levels can lead to the formation of transient or permanent bubbles at the focal region and may cause irreversible damage to the phantom material. Nevertheless, the formation of bubbles plays an important role in HIFU ablation and can be useful in certain cases [7,18]. For example, bubbles present at the HIFU focus may be the key reason for the formation of tadpole-shaped lesions in target tissues. Moreover, bubbles may act as scatters which help to identify the position of the HIFU focus or thermal lesion in an acoustically transparent hydrogel phantom (e.g., NIPAM-52 phantom). Since the formation of cavitation and/or permanent bubbles is typical in clinical HIFU, the transparent, reusable NIPAM-based hydrogel phantom system developed in this study is therefore an useful tool for the study of the interaction between bubble and lesion formation during high-power short-exposure HIFU ablations.

The fabrication and characterization of reusable transparent NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms, designed to model the formation of thermal lesions in different tissues above a threshold temperature, have been demonstrated. With identical HIFU ablation conditions, the thermal lesions formed in the AAc-controlled NIPAM-based phantoms matched well with those formed in the pork tenderloin and swine brain in terms of the overall size and shape. By adjusting the AAc concentration, the NIPAM-based phantoms were able to simulate the acoustic and thermal properties of different swine tissues, and as a result, producing similar HIFU ablation results. The NIPAM-based hydrogel phantoms developed in this study possess several major advantages over the traditional BSA- or egg-white-based phantoms; these include: transparent for real-time observation, reusable for multiple modeling, and most importantly, tailorable properties to match target tissues. It is therefore believed that they are practical tools for characterizing an ablative device (or treatment) to determine its efficacy and safety before performing actual ablations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Health Research Institutes (ME-101-PP-03) and the Ministry of Science and Technology (103-2221-E-033-025-), Republic of China (Taiwan).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ablative approaches to the minimally invasive treatment of breast cancer
Thermal ablation therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma
Other thermal ablation techniques: microwave and interstitial laser ablation of liver tumors
Percutaneous local ablative therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma: a review and look into the future
Radiofrequency thermal ablation (RFA) after transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) as a combined therapy for unresectable non-early hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
Measurements of bubble-enhanced heating from focused, MHz-frequency ultrasound in a tissue-mimicking material
Gel phantom for use in high-intensity focused ultrasound dosimetry
Magnetic resonance imaging of thermal coagulation effects in a phantom for calibrating thermal therapy devices
A tissue mimicking polyacrylamide hydrogel phantom for visualizing thermal lesions generated by high intensity focused ultrasound
Polyacrylamide gel containing egg white as new model for irradiation experiments using focused ultrasound
Reusable ultrasonic tissue mimicking hydrogels containing nonionic surface-active agents for visualizing thermal lesions
Thermoreversible copolymer gels for extracellular matrix
The application of thermo-sensitive hydrogels on ultrasound thermal therapy
Acrylic acid controlled reusable temperature-sensitive hydrogel phantoms for thermal ablation therapy
Modeling of high-intensity focused ultrasound-induced lesions in the presence of cavitation bubbles
